--- Page 1 ---
1
Load Balanced ISAC Systems for URLLC Users
Shivani Singh, Amudheesan Nakkeeran, Prem Singh, Ekant Sharma, and Jyotsna Bapat
AbstractŌĆöThis paper presents an energy-ef’¼ücient downlink
cell-free massive multiple-input multiple-output (CF-mMIMO)
integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) network that
serves ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC) users
while
simultaneously
detecting
a
target.
We
propose
a
load-balancing algorithm that minimizes the total network power
consumption; including transmit power, ’¼üxed static power, and
traf’¼üc-dependent front haul power at the access points (APs)
without degrading system performance. To this end, we formulate
a mixed-integer non-convex optimization problem and introduce
an iterative joint power allocation and AP load balancing
(JPALB) algorithm. The algorithm aims to reduce total power
usage while meeting both the communication quality-of-service
(QoS) requirements of URLLC users and the sensing QoS
needed for target detection. Proposed JPALB algorithm for ISAC
systems was simulated with maximum-ratio transmission (MRT)
and regularized zero-forcing (RZF) precoders. Simulation results
show approximately 33% reduction in power consumption, using
JPALB algorithm compared to a baseline with no load balancing,
without compromising communication and sensing QoS require-
ments.
I. INTRODUCTION
Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) is an integral
component of the international mobile telecommunications
(IMT)-2030 report for its application in industrial automation
and vehicle-to-everything (V2X) technology [1]. It allows har-
monious coexistence of sensing and communication in a single
system, improving spectrum ef’¼üciency and reduce hardware
costs. The applications envisioned for the ISAC technology re-
quire ultra-reliable and low-latency communication (URLLC)
to enable mission-critical functions, e.g. remote control of
network-enabled industrial robots, autonomous driving [2].
Cell-Free
massive
multiple-input
multiple-output
(CF-
mMIMO) technology has been widely researched in recent
times as a viable architecture to realize multi-static ISAC
networks [2]ŌĆō[11]. Reference [3] developed a maximum apos-
teriori ratio test detector for detecting the presence of a target
at a known location and proposed a downlink power allocation
algorithm to maximize the sensing signal-to-interference-plus-
noise-ratio (SINR) in a CF-mMIMO ISAC system while meet-
ing communication requirements. In [4], the authors studied
an uplink sensing scenario in a CF-mMIMO ISAC network
where the user equipment (UEs) adopt a multibeam scheme
for communication and sensing symbols, designed using sin-
gular value decomposition (SVD) beamforming and angular
beamforming. The authors in [5] formulated and solved a max-
min fairness problem where the minimum spectral ef’¼üciency
(SE) of UEs is maximized subject to per-access point (AP)
Shivani Singh, Prem Singh, and Jyotsna Bapat are with the ECE department
at IIIT Bangalore. Amudheesan Nakkeeran is with IIITB COMET Foundation
and the ECE department at IIIT Bangalore. Ekant Sharma is with the ECE
department at IIT Roorkee.
power budget and mainlobe-to-average-sidelobe ratio (MASR)
constraints by power allocation and AP mode selection (com-
munication or sensing) in a CF-mMIMO ISAC network.
The authors in [6] extended the work to consider multiple
targets and partial zero-forcing (PZF) precoders. Reference [7]
proposed various beamforming approaches to maximize the
sensing SINR in a CF-mMIMO ISAC network while ensuring
a minimum required communication SINR. In [8], the authors
performed joint location and velocity estimation of a target
in a CF-mMIMO ISAC system and derive relevant Cramer-
Rao lower bounds (CRLB), while the authors of [9] derive
closed form expressions for achievable communication rate
and CRLB for a targetŌĆÖs angle estimation.
While the aforementioned works consider an in’¼ünite block-
length regime1, indicating a focus on enhanced mobile broad-
band (eMBB) scenario, a few recent works have also in-
vestigated the ’¼ünite blocklength regime suitable serving for
URLLC users using CF-mMIMO ISAC networks. Reference
[2] proposes a successive convex approximation (SCA) based
power allocation algorithm to maximize the energy ef’¼üciency
of a CF-mMIMO ISAC network while ensuring URLLC
reliability and sensing SINR requirements. However, they have
not considered energy-ef’¼ücient load balancing to reduce power
consumption outside the peak traf’¼üc hours. The authors in [10]
considered a bistatic MIMO ISAC network where URLLC
transmissions are triggered when the presence of a target is
detected through copies of eMBB transmissions re’¼éected by
the target.
Since future networks are expected to support almost a
trillion devices [1], network power consumption has become
an important design factor [13], [14]. In [13], the authors
proposed low-complexity algorithms to decide which APs
to turn off in a CF-mMIMO network while guaranteeing
SE requirements. Reference [14] considered a CF-mMIMO
network running on an open radio access network (ORAN)
architecture and performed end-to-end resource orchestration,
including turning off a subset of APs while maximizing sum
SE. Note that [13], [14] did not consider combination of ISAC
and URLLC UEs, which is required for applications such as
autonomous driving that harness the bene’¼üts of both ISAC and
URLLC. It is therefore imperative to develop solutions that
reduce the network power consumption while still ensuring
the required communication and sensing requirements for CF-
mMIMO ISAC system in the existance of URLLC UEs. In this
context, we summarize the contributions of our paper now.
ŌĆó We introduce a model that considers minimization of
power consumption by turning communication APs off
1Shannon capacity requires in’¼ünite blocklength codewords for perfect reli-
ability in the non-asymptotic regime, which is not appropriate for achievable
data rate under URLLC constraints like transmission latency and ’¼ünite packet
length [12].
arXiv:2601.16495v1  [cs.IT]  23 Jan 2026


--- Page 2 ---
2
(load balancing) in cell-free massive MIMO ISAC sys-
tems, simultaneously serve URLLC users and detecting
target. The power consumption model includes the trans-
mit power, ’¼üxed static power, and traf’¼üc-dependent power
consumed by the APs in a CF-mMIMO ISAC network.
ŌĆó Forming the load balancing problem as a mixed integer
non-convex optimization problem, we design an iterative
algorithm based on the difference of convex functions
and convex-concave methods to jointly perform power
allocation and communication APs load balancing while
meeting the URLLC rate and sensing SINR constraints.
ŌĆó We illustrate the ef’¼ücacy of our algorithm using simu-
lations by comparing it to a baseline case which per-
forms only power allocation without any load balancing,
demonstrating that our algorithm leads to Ōł╝33% reduc-
tion in total average power consumed in the network.
II. SYSTEM MODEL
We consider a CF mMIMO multi-static ISAC system,
wherein a set K of K communication access points (APs)
collaborate via a central processing unit (CPU) to serve a set U
of U single-antenna ultra-reliable low-latency communications
(URLLC) user equipment (UEs), by leveraging the cloud
radio access network (C-RAN) architecture. Additionally, a
set R of R APs act as sensing (or receive) APs to detect
the presence of a target at a speci’¼üc location by using the
downlink communication signals re’¼éected off the target. All
the K + R APs are equipped with M antennas each and
are connected via fronthaul links to the CPU which provides
perfect synchronization among the APs. We assume time
division duplex (TDD) protocol and block-fading channel
model, in which each coherence block contains Žä symbols, out
of which Žäd symbols are used for downlink data transmission
and the rest for pilot transmission. During each coherence time
interval, the receive APs collect the re’¼éection of the Žä symbols
off the target for sensing. Since URLLC traf’¼üc is latency-
sensitive, we consider the case where the transmitted packets
(codewords) span Žäd symbols and can thus be transmitted in
one coherence period [2].
A. Communication model
We consider downlink data transmission from a load balanc-
ing perspective where, at any given time instant, only a subset
A ŌŖåK of communication APs can actively serve the UEs
while the remaining communication APs in the set K\A can
enter sleep mode to reduce power consumption. Note that the
decision of which communication APs should be included in
A impacts not just the communication quality of service (QoS)
of the UEs, but also the accuracy of target detection by sensing
APs since only a subset of communication APs are active in
the downlink. Furthermore, the location of these active APs
in’¼éuences both the communication SINR and sensing SINR,
and hence the performance of the system as a whole. The CPU
decides the form-factor of A, and we capture this through
binary indicators {╬▒k}kŌłłK as
╬▒k =
(
1,
k ŌłłA ŌŖåK,
0,
otherwise.
(1)
The communication AP activity (active/sleep) indicators
{╬▒k}kŌłłK
can be communicated to the APs via fron-
thaul interfaces. The CPU also designs, for each coher-
ence time interval, AP-speci’¼üc unit-norm precoding vectors

wi,k ŌłłCM├Ś1	
iŌłłU,kŌłłA for AP k and UE i, and UE-speci’¼üc
power coef’¼ücients
ŌłÜŽüi,k
	
iŌłłU,kŌłłK for AP k and UE i. The
transmitted signal from AP k ŌłłK at time instance l is
xk[l] =
X

(2)
where si[l] is a zero-mean unit-power communication symbol
intended for UE i. In (2), Wk = [w1,k, . . . , wU,k] ŌłłCM├ŚU is
the precoding matrix, Ds = diag{s1, . . . , sU} ŌłłCU├ŚU is the
data matrix and ŽüŌĆ▓

the power coef’¼ücient vector.
1) Power consumption: From (2), we calculate the average
transmit power of AP k ŌłłK, when it is active, as
Pk = E

a power budget of Pmax Watts. The overall power consumption
of the network is then given by [13]

where ╬Ę is the power ampli’¼üer ef’¼üciency, P0 is the static power
consumption of an AP even when it is not transmitting any
signal, P tra
k
is the traf’¼üc-dependent fronthaul power consumed
by an AP, B is the system bandwidth, and Ri is the com-
munication rate of UE i. Here, the static power consumption
at each AP is modeled as P0 = P fh
’¼üxed + PhwM [13], where
P fh
’¼üxed is the traf’¼üc-independent ’¼üxed power consumed by each
fronthaul link, and Phw is the hardware power consumption by
each antenna.
We denote the channel between communication AP k and
UE i by hi,k ŌłłCM├Ś1, which is modeled with Rayleigh fading
as hi,k Ōł╝NC (0, ╬▓i,kIM) with ╬▓i,k being the corresponding
large-scale fading coef’¼ücient. The signal received at UE i after
combining with the channel hi,k can be written as

is the inverse of the Gaussian-Q function, and Vi = 1 ŌłÆ

B. Sensing Model
Recall that the system has a set R of R sensing APs
that are involved in multi-static target detection by collecting
all communication signal from all active communication APs
and their re’¼éections through the target (if present). Taking
advantage of the C-RAN architecture, the sensing APs can
remove the known target-free components of the received
signal. The resulting received signal yr ŌłłCM├Ś1 at sensing
AP r in time instance l is given by
yr[l] =
X
kŌłłK
Žēr,ka (Žår, ╬Ėr) aT (ŽĢk, Žæk) xk[l]
|
{z
}
Re’¼éections off target
+
X
kŌłłK
Hr,kxk[l]
|
{z
}
Undesired re’¼éections by clutter
+nr[l],
(9)
where nr[l] Ōł╝NC(0,Žā2nIM) is the noise at sensing AP r
and Žēr,k Ōł╝NC
␀0, Žā2
rcs

is the radar-cross section (RCS) of
the target that includes channel gain of the path through the
target between communication AP k and sensing AP r and
follows Swerling-I model [2]. The matrix Hr,k ŌłłCM├ŚM
models environmental clutter between communication AP k
and sensing AP r.
III. PROBLEM FORMULATION AND PROPOSED SOLUTION
The CPU collects all the {yr[l]}rŌłłR from the sensing
APs across all the Žäd communication symbols in the sensing
duration and calculates the sensing SINR as
SINRs =
Žā2
rcs
PŽäd
l=1
P
rŌłłR
P
kŌłłK
h
(ŽüŌĆ▓
k)T Ar,k[l]ŽüŌĆ▓
k
i
PŽäd
l=1
P
rŌłłR
P
kŌłłK
h
(ŽüŌĆ▓
k)T Br,k[l]ŽüŌĆ▓
k
i
+ ŽädMRŽā2n
,
where
the
positive
semide’¼ünite
matrices
Ar,k[l]
and
Br,k[l], ŌłĆr ŌłłR, k ŌłłK, l = 1, . . . , Žäd are computed similar
to [3] as
Ar,k[l] = DH
s [l]WH
kaŌłŚ(ŽĢk, Žæk) aH (Žår, ╬Ėr)
├Ś a (Žår, ╬Ėr) aT (ŽĢk, Žæk) WkDs[l], (10)
Br,k[l] = DH
s [l]tr
␀Rrx,(r,k)

WH
kRT
tx,(k,r)WkDs[l].
(11)
Here Rrx,(r,k) is the spatial correlation matrix associated
with sensing AP r in the direction of communication AP k,
and Rtx,(k,r) is the spatial correlation matrix associated with
communication AP k in the direction of sensing AP r.
We aim to minimize the total power consumed in the cell-
free massive MIMO ISAC network by choosing the power
allocation vectors {Žü} and communication AP activity indica-
tors {╬▒k}kŌłłK such that constraints on URLLC downlink data
rate, sensing SINR, and per-communication-AP power budget
are satis’¼üed. This optimization problem, with variables Žü Ōēź0
and {╬▒k}kŌłłK, is formulated as follows
minimize
Ptotal ({╬▒k}kŌłłK, {Žüi,k}iŌłłU,kŌłłK)
(12a)
subject to
Ri ŌēźRmin, ŌłĆi ŌłłU,
(12b)
SINRs Ōēź╬ōsen
th ,
(12c)
Pk ŌēżPmax,
ŌłĆk ŌłłA,
(12d)
╬▒k Ōłł{0, 1},
ŌłĆk ŌłłK.
(12e)
The optimization problem in (12) is a mixed-integer non-
convex problem (MINCP), where (12b) ensures that the
URLLC data rate exceeds a minimum threshold Rmin, (12c)
is the sensing SINR constraint that ensures target detection,
(12d) is the per-communication-AP power constraint, and
(12e) restricts the communication AP activity indicators to be
either 0 (turned off) or 1 (active). Note that (12b), through (8),
ensures that the required reliability is met. Since the packets
are assumed to be transmitted within one coherence period,
the latency requirements are already met.
A.
Proposed Solution
To solve the problem in (12), we ’¼ürst deal with the
constraints, followed by designing an iterative joint power
allocation and AP load balancing (JPALB) algorithm based on
convex-concave and difference of convex functions methods.
1) URLLC data rate constraints: To overcome the dif’¼üculty
in dealing with the non-convex expression for the URLLC rate
in (8), since Vi < 1, we ’¼ürst lower bound the data rate as
Ri Ōēź
Žäd
Žä ln(2) ln(1 + ╬│i) ŌłÆ
ŌłÜŽädQŌłÆ1 (Ū½i)
Žä ln(2)
Ōē£Rlb
i .
(13)
Then, we constrain Rlb
i
ŌēźRmin instead of constraining the
actual rate as in (12b). Using (13), we arrive at the following
equivalent constraint on the SINR at UE i
╬│i Ōēźexp
 1
ŌłÜŽäd
Žä ln(2)
ŌłÜŽäd
Rmin + QŌłÆ1(Ū½i)

ŌłÆ1 Ōē£╬│th.
(14)
We then rewrite (14), using (6), as a convex second-order cone
(SOC) constraint, similar to [14], as

h
C
1
2
i,1Žü1, C
1
2
i,2Žü2, . . . C
1
2
i,UŽüU, Žān
i Ōēż
s
|bT
i Žüi|2
╬│th
,
ŌłĆi ŌłłU.
(15)
2) Sensing SINR constraints: We ’¼ürst express the sensing
SINR constraints in (12c) as
ŽüT (╬ōsen
th B ŌłÆA) Žü ŌēżŌłÆ╬ōsen
th ŽädMRŽā2
n,
(16)
where
Žü
=
h
(ŽüŌĆ▓
1)T , (ŽüŌĆ▓
2)T , . . . , (ŽüŌĆ▓
K)TiT
,
and
A
=
blkdiag {A1, A2, . . . , AK}, B = blkdiag {B1, B2, . . . , BK}

4
with
Ak
=
PŽäd
l=1
P
rŌłłR Ar,k[l]
and
Bk
=
PŽäd
l=1
P
rŌłłR Br,k[l].
We
then
use
a
linear
restriction
around a feasible point Žü(c) to arrive at the convex constraint
╬ōsen
th ŽüTBŽü + 2R

Žü(c)T
(ŌłÆA)Žü

ŌēżŌłÆ╬ōsen
th ŽädMRŽā2
n +

Žü(c)T
(ŌłÆA)Žü(c).
(17)
3) Per-communication-AP power budget constraints: We
rewrite the per-communication-AP power budget constraints
in (12d) as convex SOC constraints, similar to [3], as
ŌłźGT
kŽüŌłźŌēż╬▒k
p
Pmax,
(18)
where Gk = diag {Ōłźw1,kŌłźF, Ōłźw2,kŌłźF, . . . , ŌłźwU,kŌłźF}.
4) AP activity indicator (binary) constraints: To address
the non-convexity of the binary constraints in (12e), we ’¼ürst
rewrite it as ╬▒k ŌłÆ╬▒2
k[m] Ōēż0 with 0 Ōēż╬▒k Ōēż1, and then
use the second-order Taylor approximation for ╬▒2
k around a
feasible point ╬▒(c)
k
to arrive at, ŌłĆk ŌłłK,
0 Ōēż╬▒k Ōēż1,
(19)
╬▒k ŌłÆ

╬▒(c)
k
2
+ 2╬▒(c)
k

╬▒k ŌłÆ╬▒(c)
k

Ōēż0.
(20)
5) Convergence issues due to the relaxed binary con-
straints: To ensure that the {╬▒k}kŌłłK all converge to either
0 or 1, we add the left-hand side of (20) to the objective
function in (12a) to make it a difference of convex functions.
This allows us to use the convex-concave procedure which
iteratively solves a sequence of convex optimization problems
to arrive at the solution. The solutions obtained in iteration c
can be expressed via the previous iterates as
Žü(c),
n
╬▒(c)
k
o
kŌłłK = arg
min
Žü,{╬▒k}kŌłłK
(
Ptotal +
X
kŌłłK

╬▒k ŌłÆ

╬▒(cŌłÆ1)
k
2
+ 2╬▒(cŌłÆ1)
k

╬▒k ŌłÆ╬▒(cŌłÆ1)
k
)
.
(21)
subject to the constraints in (15), (17), (18), (19), (20).
Algorithm 1 Proposed Joint Power Allocation and AP Load
Balancing (JPALB) Algorithm
1: Initialize:
2:
Set arbitrary feasible points for Žü(0), and
n
╬▒(0)
k
o
kŌłłK.
3:
Set iteration counter c = 0.
4:
Set tolerance for stopping condition ╬Ę = 10ŌłÆ3, and
initial value of objective function f (0).
5: While c < cm or |f (c) ŌłÆf (cŌłÆ1)| > ╬Ę do:
6:
c ŌåÉc + 1.
7:
Obtain Žü(c), and
n
╬▒(c)
k
o
kŌłłK as the solution of (21)
subject to the constraints in (15), (17), (18), (19), (20).
8: End
9: Output: Power allocation vectors ŽüŌłŚ, and communication
AP activity indicators {╬▒ŌłŚ
k}kŌłłK.
6) Iterative JPALB Algorithm: We now present the pro-
posed iterative JPALB scheme in Algorithm 1, to solve the
optimization problem in (12). The loop between lines 5 and
8 in the c-th iteration generates Žü(c) and
n
╬▒(c)
k
o
kŌłłK by
solving the optimization problem in (21) in each iteration,
subject to the constraints in (15), (17), (18), (19), (20). The
algorithm outputs the power allocation vectors ŽüŌłŚand the
communication AP activity indicators {╬▒ŌłŚ
k}kŌłłK when either
the absolute difference between the values of the objective
function in successive iterations is within a tolerable limit, or
if the number of iterations exceeds a prede’¼üned maximum.
IV. NUMERICAL RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
We simulate the results for a cell-free MIMO ISAC setup
in which AP locations are generated uniformly at random in
a 500 m ├Ś 500 m area. The R closest APs to the target,
which appears at the center of the area, are designated as
the sensing APs. We consider K = 32 communication APs,
R = 2 sensing APs, and U = 8 UEs unless stated otherwise.
Each AP has M = 4 antennas and maximum transmit power
Pmax = 1 W. The carrier frequency, bandwidth, and noise
power are chosen to be 1.9 GHz, 20 MHz and ŌłÆ94 dBm
respectively. Unless stated otherwise, the minimum required
URLLC rate is Rmin = 1 b/s/Hz, the decoding error probability
for URLLC transmissions is Ū½i = 10ŌłÆ5 ŌłĆi ŌłłU, and the
sensing SINR threshold is ╬ōsen
th
= 6 dB. The power con-
sumption model parameters are in accordance with [13]: each
antenna consumes 0.2 W with a ’¼üxed fronthaul link power
consumption of 0.825 W for a total static-power consumption
per AP of P0 = 4.825 W. The traf’¼üc-dependent fronthaul
power is P tra
k
= 0.25 W/Gbps. The results are averaged
across 100 setups of uniformly random UE positions. We
use the maximum ratio transmission (MRT) and regularized
zero-forcing (RZF) precoders to show the ef’¼ücacy of the
proposed JPALB algorithm. We benchmark our results against
a baseline problem that minimizes power consumption subject
to URLLC rate requirements, sensing SINR constraints, and
per-communication-AP power budget constraints, with no load
balancing (NLB).
In Fig. 1a, we plot the empirical cumulative distribution
function (CDF) of the total power consumed (transmission
power, static power consumption, and traf’¼üc-dependent fron-
thaul power consumption) in the network for the MRT and
RZF precoding schemes, averaged across 100 UE positions.
In the case of NLB, the average power consumed is 57.95 W
for MRT and 58 W for RZF. The proposed JAPLB algorithm
with MRT and RZF requires an average power of 38.74 W and
38.9 W respectively leading to a Ōł╝33% decrease in power
consumption in comparison to their NLB counterparts. These
average values are calculated as the expected values using the
empirical CDF in Fig. 1a. The reason for the total power
consumed in the network to go down is that the proposed
JPALB algorithm switches off those communication APs that
do not contribute much towards meeting the URLLC rate and
sensing SINR requirements. In particular, the proposed JPALB
algorithm turns off Ōł╝37.5% of the communication APs in
the network when compared to the baseline NLB case, which


--- Page 5 ---
5
(a)
(b)
(c)
Fig. 1: (a) CDF of average total power consumed [W]; (b) power consumed by various components in the network; (c) total power consumed
for varying sensing SINR (╬ōsen
th ). For all plots, K = 32, R = 2, U = 8, M = 4, Rmin = 2 b/s/Hz , ╬ōsen
th = 2 dB, Žā2
rcs = 2 dBsm.
leads to approximately 33% reduction in the average total
power consumed in the network when compared to the NLB
case. Note that this power reduction happens without com-
promising the performance, speci’¼ücally the constraints of the
optimization problem in (12) ensure that the communication
and sensing QoS requirements are always met.
The power consumed by the various components in the
network is shown in Fig. 1b. The major power consumed
comprises the traf’¼üc-independent static power consumed by
the fronthaul and the hardware power consumed by the anten-
nas in the network. When the proposed JPALB turns off APs,
the major power gains are thus achieved by saving on these
two components.
Fig. 1c shows the total power consumed in the network as
a function of sensing SINR threshold (╬ōsen
th ), showing that the
sensing requirements in the ISAC network are also satis’¼üed.
As ╬ōsen
th
increases, the power consumed increases in order to
satisfy the stronger sensing SINR constraint. This is because
more number of communication APs need to be kept on to
meet the increasing sensing SINR, as this leads to more signals
being re’¼éected off the target and captured by the R sensing
APs (where R is ’¼üxed).
V. CONCLUSION
We designed an iterative joint power allocation and load
balancing algorithm to minimize the total average power con-
sumed in a cell-free massive MIMO network serving URLLC
users and simultaneously detecting the presence of a target. For
both MRT and RZF precoders, the proposed algorithm reduces
power consumption in the network by turning off communi-
cation APs that do not contribute much towards meeting the
URLLC rate and sensing SINR requirements. The proposed
JPALB algorithm turned off Ōł╝37.5% of communication APs,
leading to a 33% reduction in total power consumed by the
network, when compared to a baseline case where the URLLC
rate and sensing requirements are met without turning off any
of the communication APs.
